item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As is the case with overly long articles, I find that format screws rarely justify their own existence as narrative exercises beyond saying "I am capable of doing this." This article is no exception. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since the format of the Containment Procedures, Description, and Addenda doesn't wrap but rather go under the image, might as well try to fix it.. Need permission from the author or whatever though.

Edit: Dammit, I thought R meant Review. I'm a freaking idiot. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… if labelling a page with this object class applies its anomalous effect to it, then why is this page labelled Kronecker? Wouldn't that make its page deny any of its anomalous effects? Or is that what the weird formatting is for? Or am I just stupid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So glad I read the discussion on this, was tentative about upvoting cause I liked the concept but thought it was a tad random mismatching of elements until I learned of the hover over text. That really brought everything together for me and helped tie loose ends. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly don't quite get the connecting between Kronecker's theories and the idea of the scip, could anyone explain it to me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep pressing Tab on the page to see all of the extra little comments. I had a giggle at "Extra dimensional space" - "No".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, i +1'd this article without realizing this. +1 +1 +1

So does it mean that SCP-2062 has been contained? That it's last moments were editing it's own article? And is it really the spirit of Kronecker or an entity that emulates him?

SCP-2062 does not affect the actual subject of any documentation, only the documentation itself.

Did the foundation give him an out with this line? He's oddly silent in that sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "God's word is omnipresent" goodness he's so full of himself! It frustrates me. Upvoted for being innovative and also making me slightly concerned and frustrated that he may have actually invaded other articles. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, hey, I finally figured out what it means!

So SCP-2062 locates articles, attempts to disprove them, and converts them to the Kronecker class. In classifying SCP-2062 as Kronecker, the Foundation forced SCP-2062 to attempt to disprove itself, resulting in the hidden "negligible notations." To some extent, SCP-2062 recognizes that it has become "the subject of [its] own theory," and its lamentations about being "the blasphemer all along" suggest that it succeeded.

The fact that its containment procedures remain intact suggests that it doesn't have a persistent memory; despite disproving itself, it continues to exist and act on its own existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you mouse over a Kronecker trigger in my sandbox, a small red box should appear at the center of your browser window (no matter how far you've scrolled the page) with Kronecker's comments inside.

Currently, there is a minor bug: if the box would appear on top of the trigger that you've moused over, then the effect breaks (because you're not mousing over the trigger anymore; you're mousing over the box).

Bear in mind I only tested it in the newest Firefox. For all I know, it's totally broken in other browsers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it was boring at first, but the mouseover text really got me feeling.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Insofar, I like the edit - I'm still working on learning my CS shit, I'm only a freshman. I don't imagine you added the text in your edit, just the cursor hovering effect? At least, I didn't see the text. If that solves the issue (I never had it on my phone or laptop, so I have no idea quite what it looks like), then by all means, I'd be happy to implement it in place of my spaghetti code.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anyone's wondering why this article has formatting issues:

+ explanation for the issues seen currently
- hide explanation

The article's author used an HTML block for their effect; this generates, as output, an IFRAME, which the site CSS sizes to width 100%; as such, every paragraph containing a Kronecker remark will be shoved below the article's image due to word wrapping. Unfortunately, the HTML blocks are needed for the article's format screw to work as currently designed, because Wikidot would otherwise wipe the TITLE attributes the author is using; furthermore, IFRAMEs cannot resize themselves based on their contents, so we can't simply remove or negate the width:100% rule.

The use of the TITLE attribute has a problem of its own: it renders the document almost nonsensical to non-sighted users. Screen-reading programs will read a tag's TITLE before a tag's contents, and both will be read in the same voice; as such, Kronecker's writing will precede the things he is replying to, and they won't be easily to tell apart from the Foundation's writing.

These are challenging problems; I can't find any way to coerce Wikidot into preserving the TITLE attributes without the use of HTML blocks, so most of the issues can't be fixed without significantly changing how the format screw is presented. I also know of no other construct that displays the same way that TITLE does without screwing with screen readers.

There is one minor defect that can be fixed: currently, you can click on the Kronecker triggers and even open them in a new tab, because they're just recolored hyperlinks. The author could replace these hyperlinks with SPANs (using TITLE attributes and "cursor:pointer" as an inline style) to fix this issue.

My sandbox (revision 28) contains my attempt at getting something working for this article. I think it's overengineered and it doesn't look "native," but hell, maybe someone else'll think it's a good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is there a big space between the Item # and the Object Class?

EDIT: It doesn't show up when I'm on my phone…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Kroenecker's ghost is finally realizing that Cantor was right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what any of the hidden commentary text is talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd be a lot more impressed with this if the hover-text was actually substantial and not just weird pseudo-philosophical wanking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To a degree. It was a weird connection of thoughts that led to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is Kronecker's rejection of the idea of infinite sets supposed to have some connection to the SCP's rejection of the idea of anomalies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A'ight, thanks for the feedback.
Tbh I
I was WAY too into the spaghetti coding to really spend enough time on the content
I'll improve that next time. :U

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't actually like this skip - at first I didn't notice the mouse-over text at all, and after I read the spoiler revealing that it was there, I did not have fun hunting for all the hidden words.
On top of that, neither the article itself nor Kronecker's words were really very interesting. The article just seems to have been a vessel for the 'format screw', and there just wasn't enough in that to get me, so to speak.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, it's irreparable. I'll glance over it some day when I fix the HTML spaghetti.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the image formatting is screwed up.

Otherwise, this is neat!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoiler stuff
You've stared into the abyss

Hover your mouse over any text in the article that is "clickable" but isn't a link, there are hidden messages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Erm… what? The article itself was alright, although I had no idea about mousing over the text until I read the comments. But the comments themselves utterly confused me. I get that Kronecker is speaking, but what does he mean by that he's become the subject of his own theory?

Until I'm able to fully understand what's going on here, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What is this witchcraft?

Neat idea. Despite not being able to enjoy it on my iPhone, I quite like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately, there was no way in sight to rectify that issue. I apologize. If I ever manage to create a way to make it a little more mobile-friendly, I shall. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A big problem I have with this is it is not mobile user friendly. This should have been considered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Check the page's source code.

It's spaghetti HTML coding.
I accidentally coded it once while working on something else…
and then literally took this idea and ran with it for 45 minutes, using a far more complicated code than I should have actually used. Whoops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

I approve of this notion and it is officially headcanon, regardless of whether or not the article is changed to incorporate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*mouses over first Kronecker*

*second*

*third*

BUT THOSE TECHNICAL SKILLZ.

No, really, how did you do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh… ok. You're right. I just wanted to add an original comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hot damn, this is amazing. Fantastic meta article, excellent double format screw. (Though your picture still seems to be messed up after they fixed the site; intentional?)

Spoilers
SCP-1999

Right now, all I want to do is rewrite Elfa Loewes' date of death so that she and Herr Kronecker would have been alive at the same time. Not met, not known each other, just been alive at the same time. Enough so that people might draw conclusions…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh.
The object class and the beginning of this made me think it had something going on, but ultimately, I was disappointed. The containment procedures seem odd, either - I'd have thought they'd make a point to keeping every article in paper form, at least, to help prevent Kronecker-fuckery.

Then there's the failure to be plausible about it.

SCP-2062-1 is the IP address from which all SCP-2062 anomalies originate.

This would imply there's someone logging in the Foundation network. From a known IP address. This is easily dealt with by simply setting Foundation machines and routers to drop packets coming from there.

Triangulation of the IP address results in failure with standard positioning systems. Tracing with equipment tuned for extra dimensional topography, indicates that SCP-2062-1 originates from an extra dimensional space.

As far as I know, this is bullshit. An IP address tells you nothing about a machine's physical position. Well, I lied a little - it's possible to find out who claimed the address block it's in, and, through digging through routers and servers and stuff, find out what machine had the IP at the time. A little like trying to, say, find a person through knowing their birth number, instead of address.
What however, makes zero fucking sense, is trying to "triangulate" an IP address.
Or, in fact, an IP address somehow telling you the server is in "extradimensional space".
Even if you pulled something silly like, the address not fitting the usual [8bit].[8bit].[8bit].[8bit] format, yet somehow getting parsed and worked with fine, it'd have worked better than this, because at least what's going on would be explicitly anomalous.

EDIT: And what do you know, a quick google tells me I'm wrong. Someone has indeed found a way to get the position of a server from the ping time from three different locations, i.e. triangulation. Apparently, you can get the location to ~200km circle before having to rely onto machines located at known places within the local area's ZIP code.

While I still dislike the extradimensional IP stuff, and the skip hardly interests me, my apologies here. *takes off ushanka* =T.T=

As-is, it's a meh skip with hollywood computer science, and that's worth a downvote.

EDIT 3

So, on my way from work, I kept thinking about this, since I still dislike the "equipment tuned for extradimensional topography" bull, and overall, thought this could be done better, and wanted to do a bit of fun penance (an oxymoron, but eh) for my fuckup.

So, I did a bit of mental math.

What would have happened here - let's make it a little more interesting - is that attempting to triangulate the point in question resulted in getting, well, rather inconsistent sets of packet travel times, and thus, distances, even given the 200km allowance. Plus, trying the usual IP address magic I outlined earlier resulted in the IP either being used elsewhere irrelevant, or, plain being unassigned.

Then, one of the researchers had a clever idea. Perhaps out of boredom.

She worked out the distances from four points using the algorithm, then plugged the locations of the servers in question in, and solved for a set of four coordinates, where the servers' were set to (x,y,z,0). She got a result. She tried with a different four, and within allowed parameters, she got the same result.
Nobody's quite sure what to make of it yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this isn't a commentary on why articles are deleted. I'm not entirely sure why people are interpreting it like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Articles get deleted because they are downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the kind of format screw that I like. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2062 is an anomalous Object Class known as "Kronecker"

Upvoted

Now to read the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One note, the format is wonky mate. Anyways, nice job on the coding magic and the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is a good reason to explain why SCPs are deleted in the first place. Also, this is sort of terrifying. Some of the things on this site do not need to be kept unwatched. The Foundation has a tough job already and you just made it so much harder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the idea when Tag was flogging it, and I really like this. And the notes sound pretty realistic for him, although a tad hard to find, I must say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw man, I can't believe you actually wrote Kronecker. Good job, Paro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… Kronecker-class items should appear everywhere soon. I recommend next April's Fools day is Kronecker Day.

+1

Edit: However, "Reverting these changes have been unsuccessful" -> Has?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WHAT?! TWO REALLY GOOD FORMAT SCREW ARTICLES IN ONE DAY?! This has to be some sort of weird coincidence! o_0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This almost seems like a legitimate skip in of itself now that it's broken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It appears the anomaly was so pleased to be already written into a piece of documentation that it broke its own image formatting. Shame, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …wow. I started out loathing this 'guy' because he acts not unlike your garden-variety fundamentalist conspiracy-theorist, but towards the end when he realized that he wasn't…himself I suppose, I was actually rather saddened. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Leopold Kronecker (Lifespan: 7 December 1823 – 29 December 1891)

Couldn't this be:

Leopold Kronecker (b. 7 December 1823 – d. 29 December 1891)

or something similar? I don't think I've seen dates for people's lives formatted like that.

I like this a lot and upvoted. My only complaint is how horribly laggy it makes my computer in order to load everything (it takes a good 15 - 20 seconds to load the page). I'm placing the blame on Wikidot for this, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kudos to Fantem, TwistedGears, and A_Random_Day for beta testing.

Thanks to Tagliafierro for the spark.

Spoilers
u r bad

Try mousing over some of the text, like the word "Kronecker" in the Object Class!
Just hold your mouse over it for a moment and the hidden text will show.

<May fail on Macs. Unsure. A_Random_Day couldn't get it to go. Refer to page source if so. Warning: spaghetti coding.>
